architecture.summary: SecCodePRM is a process reward model that assigns context-aware, step-level security scores to code trajectories. It operates at the granularity of discrete code steps (separated by double newlines), evaluates partial and complete code by conditioning each step's prediction on preceding context, and uses a classification head to produce a sequence of security probabilities. The model learns from step-level supervision labels derived from static analyzers (e.g., CodeQL) and expert annotations. Information flows from code input → step tokenization → transformer backbone (Qwen2.5-Coder-7B) → classification head → per-step safety logit margins → aggregated or ranked for either full-code vulnerability detection (risk-sensitive aggregation) or safe code generation (inference-time scaling via candidate ranking).
trained_components: step-level label derivation pipeline (contrastive labeling, propagation heuristics)

biblio.title: SecCodePRM: A Process Reward Model for Code Security

claims.statement: SecCodePRM outperforms LLMxCPG on SVEN by a margin of 12%
On PrimeVul paired evaluation, SecCodePRM achieves 93.66% Pair-wise Correct Prediction (P-C) with 0% Pair-wise Vulnerable (P-V) and 0% Pair-wise Reversed (P-R)
On PrimeVul full-code classification, SecCodePRM achieves 96.83% accuracy and 96.73 F1, compared to 72.50% accuracy and 62.06 F1 for LLMxCPG
On PreciseBugs (20,000+ token examples), SecCodePRM achieves 0.59 F1, outperforming MSIVD (0.48 F1) and other baselines
On partial-code vulnerability detection, SecCodePRM achieves 100.00 F1 on PreciseBugs and maintains robust F1 across SVEN, PrimeVul, and ReposVul, outperforming models up to 17 times its size
On safe code generation with CWEval, SecCodePRM achieves 11.09% increase on func@1 and 12.98% increase on safe@1 compared to baseline
SecCodePRM accuracy remains stable even as token counts increase significantly, mitigating the 'lost in the middle' or signal-decay issues common in traditional sequence classification models

On LiveCodeBench general competitive programming, SecCodePRM mostly maintains or even improves general functionality, with QC2.5-32B achieving 55.19% pass@1, outperforming both probability-based ranking (53.23%) and QwenPRM (53.23%), suggesting security and functionality are synergistic dimensions in the coding domain
